title: Reading your water meter
subtitle: Finding it, reading the dials, and using an overnight read to detect leaks
excerpt: A water meter reads in cubic metres (1 m³ = 1,000 litres). Most UK household meters are in a small plastic box set into the pavement at the property boundary, though some are internal. Taking two readings eight hours apart with no water used gives a leak check that catches a faulty fill valve, a dripping pipe, or a running tap.
sourceNotes: Water UK metering guidance. OFWAT water meter reading and billing guidance. Waterwise household water audit methodology.

content.text: UK water meters register in cubic metres (m³). On a standard meter, the black digits on a white background are the whole cubic metres used; the red digits on a red background are the decimal fractions. 1 m³ = 1,000 litres. A typical household uses 0.3-0.5 m³ per person per day.

External: most meters are in a small plastic box (buffalo box) set flush in the pavement or in the front garden, near the property boundary. The lid usually says 'Water' and lifts with a coin or screwdriver.
Internal: flats, homes on internal risers, and some older homes have the meter inside. Common spots are under the kitchen sink, in the basement, or in a shared meter cupboard.
If you cannot locate the meter, contact your water company with your address, they can confirm the location.

Lift the cover and remove any water with a cloth or paper if it has collected on top of the meter face. Read the black digits from left to right (these are the figures on your bill). Note the date and time. Submit to your water company via their app or website if self-reading. Do not submit the red digits, these are sub-unit fractions not used for billing.

To check for a concealed leak: take a meter reading last thing at night (note the full reading including red digits). Do not use any water overnight. Take a second reading in the morning. If the reading has increased, water is flowing somewhere, either a leaking toilet cistern, a dripping outdoor tap, or a burst pipe.

A common culprit is the toilet fill valve (syphon ballcock). To isolate: close the stop tap under the toilet cistern and repeat the overnight test. If consumption drops to zero, the cistern is the source. Check the flapper valve and fill valve, both are replaceable without a plumber.

Metered water bills charge for supply (the water delivered to you) and sewerage (the water assumed to drain from your property, usually assumed equal to supply). Combined rates average .00-1.80 per m³ in England and Wales depending on your water company. Your bill should show the volume used since the previous read and the tariff applied.
